speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number 5. Today's date is March 27, 1996. We're conducting an interview with the survivor Mr. Marvin Zborowski. Mr. Zborowski, now that you have found that hard-earned money of your father's that he saved there for your family, you found it, what's happening next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next, it was quite difficult even having possessions. How do you make a living? I want to go to school. I want to be somebody. And I have to earn some money to support my mother. I didn't have my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I started to go on markets, so to say, and to earn a living. I was waiting till after the high holidays and to make up my decision, what to do. To follow, to go on the road what my brother and sister went, to Palestine, or what, actually, it's going to be. The day before the high holidays, we were still in our house. There were two Jewish families in the town that survived, that they came back and made their home in that town. Nobody from that town came back. Very few from the 4,000 Jews survived. I have no boy or girl of my age that survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Quite difficult to make your home in that home. Regardless of saying, the night, or the day before the high holidays, not erev, a day before them. Used to say practically two days before the high holidays, on Rosh Hashanah, grenades were thrown into our house, destroying the house and destroying the other house, the two Jewish homes that the Jews survived. That gave us indication, you're not wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who threw the grenades?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I would know who threw the grenades, I would definitely try to prosecute him. But I can tell you, rest assured, that they were Poles. There was no more Germans. And we realize that this is not our place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: So the safest place was that the Jews tried to congregate or to live in areas in bigger towns. And up till this day, I consider that Poland is a forbidden country to live, a Jew to live in a provincial town. If there are Jews today in Poland, they are only there in Warsaw, Krakow, and another few, Lodz, perhaps, but not in other towns.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we moved at that time, expelled, before the high holidays. We moved into Sosnowiec. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Over there, we stayed a few months, and we started to move to the border area and to run away. So where-- which border did you cross?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tried to cross a few borders, and I was caught. And I know that time is limited. And I will just mention the border that I crossed and I succeeded to get into Leipzig, to Germany, to East Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you-- which borders did you have to cross to get to Leipzig?

speaker: interviewee
text: We crossed, we crossed the border at Gorlitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we crossed the border there to Dresden. From Dresden, we went to Leipzig. From Leipzig, we had to cross to West Germany. And West Germany, we arrived the beginning of 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Being that my brother was already in Feldafing-- this was a displaced person camp for Jews, and I joined my brother in that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: I assume, then, that you were in touch with your brother, since you said you knew where your brother was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, by mail.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, describe your stay at Feldafing. Did you finally go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In Feldafing, we lived in a villa, so to say. One room. There was-- in that villa was former German family lived before, and the rooms were divided and given to each family a room. Being in that room, I registered, and I applied, and I was going to a vocational school. And I felt that the only way to do it is to learn a trade. And I was starting to apply, and I was accepted to a [INAUDIBLE] mechanical school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was, who was the head of the school? I mean, who was sponsoring--

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: --I think that this was a Jewish organization. I think it was the ORT. Now, going in that, I did not find this quite interesting. And I said, I would like rather to prepare to go through my general studies and get to higher education.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was going through speedy courses, going though school, and applying to the, to Munich in [INAUDIBLE], Germany. It's in Bavaria, which Feldafing is also part of Bavaria. And I applied to the Oskar von Miller Polytechnical, Polytechnic School of Engineering in Munich, Germany. And you were accepted?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was accepted, and I was, was one of the youngest. Went with another friend of mine. We're one of the youngest one. I also did not feel comfortable in the school, because my schoolmates were former military people. They were still wearing uniforms, without the badges, of course. I was very uncomfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were they? Were they Poles or Germans or who--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Polytechnic had a limited amount of space for students, so there we're really, actually, the one that had preference, and also the one who were actually, during the war, prosecuted or discriminated. So we got the preference. While speaking about education, I have to go back to one segment of during the war, of 1941, '42, where there were no schooling in our hometown, Zarki. And I'm happy that I'm speaking about education. And this is very, very important for me. Whereby the Jew was not allowed to go to school, my father with another few friends hired a teacher, private teacher, that came from another town, from Plotzk. And I even remember her name, Judy [NON-ENGLISH]. I even have a picture of that school class of, I think, we were six children. And she gave us courses, speedy courses, which I will definitely say that a lot of those courses still remain and being used by me today. Mainly what I did grasp or learn was the German language, which I'm fluent in it. And this helped me to get into the Polytechnic as well. This, in turn, also helped me being in the Polytechnic, getting this higher education, that I grasp, again-- I'm not saying that I do not speak with an accent. I'm not saying that I'm full command of the language. But I would definitely say that I do feel-- maybe I would-- correctly-- I would have to correct myself. I'm in command of the language of English.

speaker: interviewer
text: You certainly are. Now, how long did you stay in the Polytechnic?

speaker: interviewee
text: One year.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were studying at the Polytechnic, did you live in Feldafing in the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The, the DP camp was liquidated, I think, 1949 or 1950. The liquidation of the camp-- and I lived in Munich, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother lived, then, with you in Munich as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My mother, going back now, my sister finally arrived as an illegal immigrant to Israel, the illegal children. The first illegal immigration to, to Israel by the children, my sister was in that group.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Palestine. After the establishment of the state of Israel, obviously, my mother wanted to go to her little girl. So she emigrated in 1950 to Israel. We, in turn, wanted, also, to emigrate to Israel. It's not the question I had anything against the United States. I had nobody to claim that I should be able to go to the United States. I have no relatives in the United States. So my heart was set, or my plans were set to go to Israel. But I did want to finish my school. But at the time that I was going to school, to the Polytechnic, the Jewish organization, the HIAS, the, the American Jewish Congress and Jewish committee were constantly calling upon us to move out-- this was the Korean War-- to get out, because we are standing before a third World War, and they would really like that we should move out of Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the question was now, where do I go? At that time, of the grace of God, of course, and of the graciousness of our late President Truman, which I think he was a great humanitarian-- and I have to state, not Roosevelt. Not Roosevelt, I would underline three lines.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because Roosevelt was a great man, and as far as the Depression time, helping the American people, which he did, is right. But as far as humanity is concerned, I would say that Truman was one of the greatest presidents, and, and by establishing the Displaced Persons Act. And under that act, I was privileged to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Eli come with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eli came a few weeks later. I arrived in the United States on January 27, 1952, and Eli arrived, I think, a few weeks later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a very interesting story, the day of my arrival.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: The day of my arrival was very bleak. It was a rainy day on January 27. To arrive in New York was very, very, very uninteresting, especially, where do I go? What should I do? So my contract was, actually, that I had to go to Philadelphia. And I applied to Philadelphia, and I was happy to go to Philadelphia. Because Philadelphia, I had a friend there that, that lived in Philadelphia. I want to go to Philadelphia. I had nobody somewhere else. Of course, I had people, you know. Being that I wanted to go to Philadelphia, the person who waited for me was from the HIAS. And he said, Mr. Zborowski, you're going to Baltimore. I said, I'm not going to Baltimore. I'm going to Philadelphia. He said, no, no, no, no. You have to go to, to-- I said, look, don't start up with me. I'm a very nervous kid. I'm not going to Baltimore. I'm going to Philadelphia. You have to.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, if you have to, if you could change, then I want to change to New York. Why do you want to go to New York? All the refugees, all the foreigners, or whatever description I have. I'm very proud to say that I'm a refugee, of course. People, my friends, I keep on saying, being here in the States for 40 whatever years, you still consider yourself-- I said, that's the way I came. I came as a refugee boy. I mean, it's nothing you can do about it. So anyhow, I said, if you could change to Baltimore, you could change to New York. So why do you want to stay in New York? I said, because I have somebody here in New York. Anyhow, to make this a little bit shorter, she gave me the permission to stay in New York, but the provision that I will not ask for any assistance and any benefits from the organization, because I refused to go to the place, the destination, to Baltimore.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I do not need anything, and thank God I did not need anything. And it's a matter of fact, I paid back my passage, which I got through the organization. I paid everything 10 times over for the graciousness of bringing me back, bringing me to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: But please bring us into the picture. You are arriving in New York. You don't know anyone. You don't speak English--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --then yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you're lost on the moon.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was never scared for anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell us about you. What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I, when I, when finally, she called me away and aside, she saw that I-- she has a hard knock-- that I'm stiff-necked kid, so to say, obnoxious one. She said, you want to stay in New York. I said, yes, absolutely, yes. OK, so stay here. Be quiet. OK. I said, I will be quiet. Can I stay in New York? She said, yeah, you could stay in New York, but be quiet. I said, I will be quiet. I promise you. I said, but don't have the last word. I have the last word. OK. Anyhow, she came up to me and said, you could stay in New York, and you can go if you wish. I said, yes, I have some, some belongings. And I'm not going to walk for belongings. I don't know what means a taxi, or whatever. I said, why couldn't you take away from me whatever I have in storage, and I will take a small suitcase, and I will go with that. OK, she said, this could be granted. She took and gave me a receipt for all my belongings, how many pieces I had. And I don't have to tell you, I came as a young boy, but I, I was, like, coming like being a bachelor. Eli was married before, so whatever his wife bought for herself, she bought for me. So I came like I would be a divorced or a bachelor man, prepared with all kind of dishes and porcelains and crystals, and don't ask what I had, like I would be really equipped. And here I come, a young man, in the, so to say, in the early 20s.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, so-- but she took away from me these things, and I'm walking. She calls me back. She calls me back, and said, tell me. And I see that she is crying. She says, where are you going? Are you really know what you're doing? I said yes, I know. I said, I, I-- but you've got to walk out from this building. Where, where do you go? So I said, I will make my way. Anyhow, she said, look, you're going tonight. I told, I'm going to this address, and that address, whatever it is. So she said, look. What happens if the people will not let you sleep? Here, you have a voucher for one night. Not for two nights, for one night. I said, thank you very much. I really appreciate. I never used that voucher. I wish that I could-- Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to some friends that were nice enough. Then later on, I established myself. I worked for $30. Six months later, I was in the States. I was in business. And I am in business still to this day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first job? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first job is very interesting. I-- those days, the minimum salary was $0.75. I used to stay 40 hours. I got $30. I mean, gross income, $30. So I was not worth it more. Don't think that I was complaining. I did not complain. I don't think I was worth that $30.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you, what were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was pressing hats.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then six months later?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, then the, the man said, look, you know how to write and how to read. I mean, you don't have to press hats. I mean, we'll put you in the office. And I was seeing what he was doing. So six months later, I said, you know what, I can do better job than you do. And I bought 40 machines, and I made myself a factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in that business?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in that business, that was from 1952 to 1956, '57. Four or five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Made a lot of money--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: --those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, of course, being that I was a young man, so I married in 1954. Married Celina. Had a blind date, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you fell in love?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it worked. Funny thing is, I met her before, and I had to wait until she got really mature to be my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a few years younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went into your own business? Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we joined-- I joined with my brother. We, we went to the export business. And since that time, we are together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now tell us something about your family, your children, your grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before we are going into the family, I just would like to speak about the success of the business, and then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Please do.

speaker: interviewee
text: --just make it in one sentence. We went into the export-import business, and then, eventually, we became the distributor for the Sheaffer Pen company. And up to this day, we're still representing the Sheaffer Pen company in certain Latin American countries. We have factories in Argentina, Brazil, and Mexico. As well, we were in the, in the knitting business. Not knitting, just making-- we making-- we were making our own fibers in Brazil. We had a factory that I was involved that we had 2,000 people. We are 50% shareholder in that factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you in any other business in this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, of course, I was also involved in the construction business, a lot of homes in this area that I built, especially in the borough of Queens. And I was also, and up till this day, I'm also in the banking business. I'm a shareholder of a bank. I was representing, being in Switzerland in that Swiss bank, being on the board of the, of the bank corporation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now tell us something. Since you are so successful, how are you giving it back to society in your social work and philanthropic work?

speaker: interviewee
text: In turn, being-- thank God, being to the gracious of God-- being successful, I shared my wealth. We established an endowment. My children went to Queens College. We made an endowment to Queens College where lectures are being given in the name of Celina and Marvin Zborowski. I also am part of the Yeshiva University [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Interdisciplinary chair of Holocaust studies under the name of Diana and Eli Zborowski. I'm on the board of-- international board in Jerusalem of Yad Vashem. I'm serving on the board of directors here in the United States of the Friends of the Yad Vashem. I'm involved politically, supporting both the Republican and the Democratic Party, whereby I have no personal gain, not from one, not from the other, which is not tax-deductible, not at all. I'm involved in communal life, belonging to the synagogue, to my local synagogue. I lived in Jamaica. I'm living in Jamaica now for the last three and a half years. I lived before for 30 some odd years in Hillcrest, where I was an officer, a builder, a founder of one of the largest orthodox community-- modern orthodox communities-- in the United States. We had over 500 vibrant families in our community.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you also involved with any of the universities? Are you on, on boards of universities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm on the board of the Queens College.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you involved with any of the Holocaust, the Shoah observances or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. All, since the day, always involved in the commemoration of the Yom Hashoah. I was the one who installed the first one in our community, in Hillcrest, and it's been perpetuated every year. And also, it's being followed the same way here where I am now, in Jamaica Estates.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now would you tell us about your beautiful family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family is really a lovely family. We are very close. The children are very close to us, and we are close to the children. I have two wonderful sons. They are married. I never had the privilege of having daughters till they got married, and I have now two daughters, which-- just like daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many grandchildren do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we have three, and now one was born this week, which is four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mazel tov.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much. It's four. He is three days old, and let's hope and pray that everything will be OK, that we can go and proceed and enjoy the pleasures out of the children. The children, the older one is Mark, which he came after seven years that we were married. We always speak about it. The children were not easy. Not that we didn't plan, not that we didn't want-- we wanted children, but the first one came after seven years, so we appreciate them much, much more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Zborowski, do you feel that your Shoah experiences, the experiences during the Shoah, have influenced the way you look at the world and at people, and you view life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say the experience that I have is, first of all, for humanity. I'm a very liberal-minded person, in respect of equal rights for everybody. I do not consider myself that I am better because I accumulated, relatively speaking, a comfortable position in my life or that I accomplished a position politically or a communal or even in any endeavors. I do feel that if everybody would have gotten the possibility, and seen what I went through, education, everybody could make it, if I made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your feelings about the future for-- would you like to tell something to your children, your grandchildren, and the world at large, on the basis of your experiences? How do you view that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good question. But I will word it before, just inject one word about the past--

speaker: interviewer
text: Please do.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and to be able to get into the future. Without the past, we would not be able to continue our future. The past experience that I had with the people that I live-- and I do consider myself that I was born in Poland, that I'm a Jew born in Poland. I could not deny, and neither do I want to deny it. But I do say it, that the guilt that the Polish people have-- and they should come up, and they will eventually come up-- is because they did not have the education. They were not educated to understand to be equal with all their citizens of their country. In other words, I do say, if the Polish people would had a better education, there would not be that much hatred. The great part of it that the Polish people had-- and I want to have it on record-- the only education that the Polish people had is to go to church and to listen what the Church is saying. Therefore, the Church has a big part. Don't blame the Poles, and don't blame anybody. Blame the Church for the unfortunate. We are blessed now with a great pope which happens to be born in Poland, the country of my ancestors. But I was expecting-- he's doing a great job, fantastic job. He's the only pope that history could show that is moving, is, is movable, doing things. He's a doer.

speaker: interviewer
text: But at the same time, he should do something for humanity. And it's been proven and been repeated again, and that's where I'm getting into the future, which is going on now in the country of Yugoslavia. What kind of war is there? It's a war of the churches. Mr. Zborowski, those two wonderful Polish families whom you are so grateful to, what did you do for them in gratitude?

speaker: interviewee
text: In gratitude, we always remember them, and we'll never forget. We try to support them till this day. Of course, Mr. and Mrs. Placzek died. When I came back after 40 years to Poland, the first thing what I did, I came to town. I went to the cemetery to pay the respect to the-- Mr. and Mrs. Placzek. The other one that we were six months, we had still the privilege to see him after the war, and we took him to Jerusalem, the same way we did with the offspring of the Placzek. He had only one daughter, and till this day, we're supporting her, helping her, and we also took her to Jerusalem and to be inscribed and be planted in the Valley and in the, in the Alley of the, of the Righteous.

speaker: interviewer
text: So both-- you saw to it that both families are inscribed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Both families are recognized as such.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Zborowski, we want to thank you very much for sharing your experiences with us. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is my privilege, and by the grace of God.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Zborowski, it was obviously such a great honor to have your friends who saved so many lives, Jewish lives, have them-- have the trees planted in the Alley of the Righteous. But you were going to tell us something about your thoughts and ideas about the future.

speaker: interviewee
text: While speaking about the past, I would like to get into the present and the future. The present, what we try to do is to establish as much as possible. Like I would admire the, the graciousness of people like Spielberg and many others, who would try to perpetuate the memory of the survivors, and especially of the Kedoshim of the martyrs that they got killed. I would only say, with, with the help, now, with having the, so to say, the possibility of establishing museums or places of learning about the Holocaust, which is a great, great achievement. And I strongly feel that the, the, especially the Yad Vashem in Jerusalem, which is a remarkable and an outstanding piece of, of-- collection. And it's being-- growing and more and more, because it's, it's expanding of so much more and more material. And even having all this dates, and especially the Washington museum, and the New York City plans for museum, and there's many other towns and cities of museums, it's just-- it's good. It's [INAUDIBLE]. I'm supporting, and I'm all for it, because they should-- people should not have any doubt about it, what had really happened. And by remembering, then we definitely will know that it should not happen again. Thank you very much for giving me this opportunity, and I will try to, as long God will give me the years to live, to remember. And I could not forget the years of horror, and I will try to perpetuate for the generations to come. Thank you very much.
